name: Discord MCP Server (SaseQ)
what_it_does: Community MCP server for Discord enabling AI agents to send messages, read channels, manage servers, and interact with Discord bots — connecting agents to Discord communities.
use_cases: Sending notifications and updates to Discord channels from agents
Reading recent messages in Discord channels for context
Managing Discord server roles and permissions
Posting agent-generated content to community Discord servers
Monitoring Discord channels for triggers in agent workflows
not_for: Regulated industries requiring data residency guarantees
High-frequency automated posting (Discord rate limits are strict)
best_when: An agent needs to interact with Discord communities — sending notifications, posting updates, or reading messages for context.
avoid_when: You need enterprise-grade messaging with SLAs, audit logs, or compliance features.

auth.notes: Discord bot token with explicit OAuth2 scope selection (message reading, sending, server management). Bot must be invited to servers with specific permissions. Scopes set at bot creation.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Discord API is free for bots; Nitro subscription not required for bot use
pricing.notes: MCP server and Discord bot API are both free. No cost to build bots.

performance.rate_limits: 50 requests/second global; per-route limits apply; message sending: 5/5s per channel

known_agent_gotchas: Discord rate limits are strict and per-route — agents hitting multiple channels rapidly will get 429 responses
Bot must be manually added to each server with specific permissions — cannot programmatically join servers
Message history access requires GUILD_MESSAGES or MESSAGE_CONTENT intent — must be enabled in dev portal
Community MCP (not official Discord) — Discord API changes may break functionality without notice
Slash commands require separate registration and manifest updates
No idempotency — duplicate sends possible if agent retries on network errors
agent_readiness.security_notes: Bot tokens should be rotated regularly. Scope granularity is good (Discord's intent system). HTTPS enforced. Community MCP — audit code before production use.

security_score_components.security_notes: Discord's intent and permission system provides good scope granularity. Bot token security is straightforward. Community MCP — verify dependencies.
